coredata.dc:title: Atomic Ru-embedded three-dimensional ion-conductive tunnel networks in Ta/Ru co-doped Nb2O5 nanosheets enabled by multifunctional graphene quantum dots for ultrahigh-energy-density supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Xiaoshan, Li
Ruiyi, Li
Yongqiang, Yang
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Low conductivity and structural stability limit the practical application of Nb2O5 electrode materials in high-performance supercapacitors. The paper reports synthesis of Nb2O5 nanosheets by introducing Ta, Ru and histidine-functionalized and boron and phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dot (HBP-GQD). The synthesis includes the conversion of Nb(V), Ta(V) and Ru(III) into stable complexes via strong coordination between metal ions and HBP-GQD, and subsequently annealing in N2 at 850℃. The resulting (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD composite shows one sheet-like nanostructure with interconnected tunnel reactors embedded with Ru atoms. The coordination significantly improves the uniform distribution of Nb, Ta and Ru. The integration of Ru and HBP-GQD achieves to crystal orientation growth, 60919.54-fold increase in electrical conductivity, 309.5-fold increase in the Li+ diffusion efficiency, and 192.8-fold increase in electrochemically active area. (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD electrode generates ultrahigh specific capacitance of 1157.5F g−1 at 1 A g−1, which is more than 5.2-fold that of GQD-free variant and 3.4-fold that of Ru-free variant. The symmetric supercapacitor with (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD electrodes and Li2SO4/PVA electrolyte exhibits outstanding supercapacitor performance, including high capacitance (341.4F g-1 at 1 A g−1), high-rate capability (155.5F g-1at 10 A g−1), cycling stability (98.7 % retention after 10000-cycle), and high energy density (137.0 Wh kg−1 at 850 W kg−1).

head.ce:title: Atomic Ru-embedded three-dimensional ion-conductive tunnel networks in Ta/Ru co-doped Nb2O5 nanosheets enabled by multifunctional graphene quantum dots for ultrahigh-energy-density supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We firstly reported one strategy for synthesis of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)1O1.05 nanocomposite by introducing multifunctional graphene quantum dot. The composite electrode exhibits excellent supercapacitor performance.

Low conductivity and structural stability limit the practical application of Nb2O5 electrode materials in high-performance supercapacitors. The paper reports synthesis of Nb2O5 nanosheets by introducing Ta, Ru and histidine-functionalized and boron and phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dot (HBP-GQD). The synthesis includes the conversion of Nb(V), Ta(V) and Ru(III) into stable complexes via strong coordination between metal ions and HBP-GQD, and subsequently annealing in N2 at 850℃. The resulting (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD composite shows one sheet-like nanostructure with interconnected tunnel reactors embedded with Ru atoms. The coordination significantly improves the uniform distribution of Nb, Ta and Ru. The integration of Ru and HBP-GQD achieves to crystal orientation growth, 60919.54-fold increase in electrical conductivity, 309.5-fold increase in the Li+ diffusion efficiency, and 192.8-fold increase in electrochemically active area. (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD electrode generates ultrahigh specific capacitance of 1157.5F g−1 at 1 A g−1, which is more than 5.2-fold that of GQD-free variant and 3.4-fold that of Ru-free variant. The symmetric supercapacitor with (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD electrodes and Li2SO4/PVA electrolyte exhibits outstanding supercapacitor performance, including high capacitance (341.4F g-1 at 1 A g−1), high-rate capability (155.5F g-1at 10 A g−1), cycling stability (98.7 % retention after 10000-cycle), and high energy density (137.0 Wh kg−1 at 850 W kg−1).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors as advanced energy storage systems show a significant potential in applications ranging from electric vehicles to grid stabilization, leveraging their unparalleled power density and ultrafast charge/discharge kinetics [1]. Nevertheless, widespread commercialization is hindered by intrinsic limitations, notably low energy density (<10 Wh kg−1) and economic barriers associated with high-performance electrode materials [2]. Niobium pentoxide (Nb2O5) has garnered considerable attention as a promising electrode material due to distinctive advantages such as layered orthorhombic structure facilitating rapid ion diffusion, dominant pseudocapacitive charge storage mechanisms enabling > 90 % capacity retention after 5000 cycles at the practical current densities and earth-abundance and environmental compatibility. However, intrinsic challenges persist for Nb2O5, including low electrical conductivity necessitating conductive additives, irreversible phase transitions during deep ion insertion causing structural fatigue, and discrepancies between theoretical and practical capacities due to kinetic limitations [3–5].

To address these limitations, researchers have developed multifaceted strategies spanning structural engineering [6], composite design [7], and surface/interface optimization [8]. In structural engineering, the three-dimensional interconnected H-Nb2O5 Nanoparticles with shear structure synthesized via in situ hydrothermal methods exhibit more lithium storage sites, enabling 269 mA h g−1 at 0.02 A g−1 with 97 % capacity retention over 100 cycles, while hierarchical three-dimensional porous microspheres with “room and pillar” structures retain 49.8 % capacity at 4 A g−1 [9]. Synergistic Engineering of Phase and Cu Doping highlights monoclinic H-Cu0.1-Nb2O5 superior ion diffusion coefficient (∼10-15 cm2 s−1), one order of magnitude higher than H-Nb2O5 phase, as demonstrated in annealing the solvothermally-synthesized microscale bulk-like prismatic morphology delivering 203.6 mA h g−1 at 1 A g−1 [10]. Composite strategies integrate T-Nb2O5 with conductive matrices: graphene hybrids achieve 47 W h kg−1 via π-π bonding [11], carbon nanotube-wrapped nanofibers retain 98 % capacity over 5000-cycle [12], and core–shell structures sustain > 85 % capacity after 10000-cycle at 0.2 A g−1 [13]. Heterostructures with MXene quadruple rate performance through built-in electric fields [14]. Surface engineering further enhances stability: oxygen vacancy-rich Nb2O5 boosts capacity by 25 % with 54 % retention over 100 cycles [15], while atomic-layer-deposited Nb2O5 coatings enable > 99.4 % capacity retention across 500 cycles [16]. Complementary electrolyte innovations, such as ionic liquids (EMIMBF4), expand voltage windows to 3 V, doubling energy density [17]. Despite these advances, challenges persist: low conductivity, irreversible phase transitions during deep cycling, scalability barriers in synthesis, and a great gap between practical and theoretical capacities [18].

In the study, we present one strong coordination route for synthesis of Nb2O5 nanosheet composite (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD) for high-performance supercapacitors by introducing Ta, Ru and histidine-functionalized and boron and phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dot (HBP-GQD). The as-synthesized (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD shows sheet-like nanostructure with interconnected tunnel reactors embedded with Ru atoms, high conductivity and full exposed electrochemically active sites. The symmetrical supercapacitor with (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD electrodes and Li2SO4/PVA gel electrolyte exhibits superior supercapacitor performance and broad prospects for application in flexible electronic devices.

HBP-GQD was synthesized via a thermal annealing method [19]. In a typical procedure, 5 g of citric acid, 3.7 g of histidine (His), 0.6 g of boric acid (H3BO3), and 540 μL of 85 wt% phosphoric acid (H3PO3) were dissolved in 5 mL ultrapure water under vigorous stirring. The homogeneous mixture was evaporated to dryness at 80 ℃ to remove residual water, followed by thermal annealing at 180 ℃ for 3 h. The resulting crude product was dissolved in ultrapure water to form 200 mg mL–1 solution and then purified via centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) to remove large aggregates, followed by dialysis (1000 Da MWCO membrane for 48 h and lyophilization to obtain the final HBP-GQD powder. Control samples—GQD, H-GQD, and HB-GQD—were prepared by selectively omitting specific precursors: GQD (without His, H3BO3 and H3PO3), H-GQD (without H3BO3 and H3PO3), and HB-GQD (without H3PO3), respectively, as reported previously.

(Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD was synthesized by a wet chemical method. Niobium oxalate (C10H5NbO2·xH2O), tantalum pentachloride (TaCl5), and ruthenium chloride hydrate (RuCl3·xH2O) were mixed in a molar ratio of 0.7:0.15:0.15 and added to 60 mL of a solution containing 0.8 g HBP-GQD, 10 mL 30 wt% H2O2, and 5 mL concentrated ammonia. After 2 h of stirring, the mixture was impregnated into 1 g of pre-dried rambutan peel powder. The resulting material was vacuum-dried at 60℃ for 12 h and subsequently annealed at 850 °C for 3 h under N2 flow (heating rate: 5℃ min–1). The resulting product was washed with ultrapure water until no chloride ions were detected by the silver nitrate method. The collected samples were vacuum dried at 120 °C for 8 h to obtain the final product. Control composites—(Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5-HBP-GQD was synthesized by omitting RuCl3·xH2O and using one new Nb/Ta molar ratio of 0.7:0.3, and (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-GQD, (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-H-GQD, and (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HB-GQD—were synthesized by replacing HBP-GQD with GQD, H-GQD, or HB-GQD, respectively.

Morphological features were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL S-4800) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100). Crystallographic properties were examined via X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE, Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.15406 nm). Chemical states and surface composition were probed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Axis Supra, Al Kα source). Oxygen vacancies were quantified using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker EMX PLUS, X-band, 9.8 GHz). Additional analyses included Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw InVia, 532 nm laser), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus), FT-IR spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700), and electronic conductivity measurements (JEOL JES-FA200 with EC-RAIR-H operando cell).

(Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD integrates three metallic elements (Nb, Ta, Ru) to synergistically enhance the electrochemical performance. Niobium (Nb) serves as a primary electrode material, leveraging multi-redox activity of Nb2O5 to achieve a high specific capacitance. Its shear-aligned crystalline structure further facilitates rapid ion diffusion pathways. Tantalum (Ta) is introduced as a dopant in Nb2O5 lattice to address the structural instability issue inherent to pure Nb2O5 [20]. Despite sharing similar physicochemical properties with Nb, Ta2O5 exhibits a superior structural rigidity due to its stronger Ta-O bonding, which stabilizes the nanocrystalline framework under cycling stress [21]. In contrast, ruthenium (Ru) enhances both charge storage capacity and electrical conductivity. The substitution of Ru3+ into the Nb2O5 lattice introduces additional charge carriers, effectively lowering charge transfer resistance. During the thermal annealing, the partial reduction of Ru4+ generates metallic Ru atoms, which subsequently anchor within the interlayer tunnels of Nb2O5 nanocrystals. This unique architecture achieves near-100 % atomic utilization of Ru, maximizing its pseudocapacitive contribution. Concurrently, the interplay between Nb’s multi-redox states, Ta’s structural reinforcement, and Ru’s dual conductive/capacitive roles establishes a hierarchical energy-level distribution. Such synergistic interactions not only broaden electrochemical reaction window but also accelerate reaction kinetics, collectively boosting specific capacitance and rate capability [22].

HBP-GQD acts as a multifunctional scaffold to optimize synthesis and performance of the composite through three key mechanisms (Fig. 1 ). First, abundant edge-functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine) in the HBP-GQD enable strong coordination with Nb5+, Ta5+, and Ru3+ precursors. This coordination inhibits premature hydrolysis of metal ions in aqueous media, ensuring uniform nucleation and suppressing phase segregation caused by disparate physicochemical properties of the heterometallic species. Consequently, this leads to the formation of small Nb2O5 nanoparticles with homogeneous elemental distribution. Second, selective adsorption of HBP-GQD on the specific crystallographic planes of Nb2O5 governs anisotropic crystal growth. Unlike conventional graphene, HBP-GQD’s functionalized edges and nanoscale dimensions allow precise modulation of surface energy differences between crystal facets. This facet-selective interaction directs the assembly of Nb2O5 nanocrystals into tailored morphologies (e.g., truncated octahedrons), which are critical for exposing electrochemically active sites [23]. Third, HBP-GQD undergoes dual functional evolution during thermal annealing: Reductive role: Intrinsic reducing capacity of HBP-GQD facilitates in situ reduction of Ru3+ to metallic Ru0, which embeds atomically dispersed Ru species within the interlayer tunnels of Nb2O5. This configuration maximizes Ru’s atomic utilization (>95 %) while creating interconnected conductive pathways [24]. Structural role: Partial carbonization of the HBP-GQD generates ultrathin graphene sheets that conformally coat the Nb2O5 nanocrystals. These graphene layers enhance the interfacial electron transport due to its high conductivity, while residual hydrophilic groups (e.g., oxidized moieties) improve electrolyte wettability. The synergy between metallic Ru and graphene-modified surfaces collectively reduces charge transfer resistance and stabilizes the electrode–electrolyte interface during charge–discharge cycling.

Rambutan peel, a biomass-derived material with intrinsic hierarchical mesoporous architecture (Fig. S1), serves as a multifunctional scaffold in electrode engineering. First, its high surface area and oxygen-containing functional groups enable strong chemisorption of Me-HBP-GQD complexes (Fig. S2), effectively confining these precursors within the porous matrix during thermal annealing. This spatial confinement prevents the nanoparticle aggregation and ensures the uniform dispersion of active components. Second, upon carbonization at 850 °C under inert atmosphere, biotemplate undergoes structural evolution: The lignocellulosic framework transforms into a mechanically robust 3D carbon network, which acts as a stress buffer to accommodate volume fluctuations during ion intercalation/deintercalation. Simultaneously, the retained meso-porosity creates continuous ion diffusion channels, reducing ionic transport resistance. The conductive carbon matrix establishes percolation pathways for electrons, lowering interfacial charge transfer resistance. Third, synergistic effects emerge between carbon framework and embedded active materials: The carbon skeleton’s mechanical resilience inhibits electrode cracking during long-term cycling. The surface oxygen groups enhance electrolyte wettability, which promotes rapid ion adsorption at electrode–electrolyte interface.

The synthesis of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD includes mainly two steps. The first step is to combination of Nb(V), Ta(V) and Ru(III) with HBP-GQDs to form stable Me-HBP-GQD complex. The second step is to thermal annealing in N2 atmosphere to produce (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD. The pyrolysis temperature is identified as a key determinant of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD. The results indicate that sheet-like (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD nanocrystals form at temperatures exceeding 750°C, with crystallinity improving as temperature rises. However, the particle size increases significantly above 950°C. To balance crystallinity and particle size, an optimal annealing temperature of 850 °C is selected for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD synthesis.

To optimize the doping ratios of Ta and Ru in co-doped Nb2O5 nanosheets, two distinct series of control samples were systematically synthesized: (Nb0.85-xTaxRu0.15) 2O5-HBP-GQD (Ta doping variation) and (Nb0.85-yTa0.15Ruy) 2O5-HBP-GQD (Ru doping variation). Their specific capacitances were evaluated at a current density of 1 A g–1. As depicted in Fig. S3A, the Ta doping ratio (x = 0 ∼ 0.2) exhibited negligible influence on the specific capacitance. The undoped Ta sample (x = 0) displayed a capacitance of 892.3F g–1, which gradually increased to 1157.5F g–1 at x = 0.15, followed by a slight decline to 1138.0F g–1 at x = 0.2. This non-monotonic trend suggests that excessive Ta incorporation may introduce structural distortions, thereby limiting further capacitance enhancement. Consequently, an optimal Ta doping ratio of 0.15 was selected to maximize electrochemical performance. In contrast, the Ru doping ratio demonstrated a pronounced impact on capacitance (Fig. S3B). Increasing the Ru content from y = 0 to y = 0.15 triggered a remarkable surge in capacitance from 327.2F g–1 to 1157.5F g–1, attributed to the enhanced redox activity of Ru species and improved charge transfer kinetics. However, beyond y = 0.15, the growth rate diminished significantly, likely due to saturation of active sites or kinetic limitations in ion diffusion. To balance cost efficiency (via reduced Ru usage) and high capacitance, the Ru doping ratio was likewise fixed at 0.15.

Fig. 2
A and B present SEM and TEM images of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD. The composite exhibits a well-defined two-dimensional (2D) sheet architecture. This unique morphology originates from HBP-GQD-mediated directional growth of specific crystallographic facets in Nb2O5 nanocrystals, which promotes preferential formation of ultrathin Nb2O5 nanosheets. These sheets further self-assemble into a hierarchically porous framework with micron-scale lateral dimensions and a sub-100 nm thickness. Such a micro-nano hierarchical structure offers dual advantages: (1) the maximized exposure of electrochemically active sites due to the high surface-to-volume ratio of 2D sheets, and (2) enhanced electrolyte permeability through interconnected nanopores, which synergistically facilitates rapid ion transport kinetics during electrochemical reactions.

Fig. 2 C-G present high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD, accompanied by inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) patterns and intensity profiles corresponding to lines 1 and 2 in the marked HRTEM region. Fig. 2 D reveals one well-ordered atomic arrangement devoid of interstitial impurities. Notably, Nb5+ (ionic radius: 0.64 Å) [25] and Ta5+ (0.64 Å) [26] share identical ionic radii, making their contributions indistinguishable in the intensity profiles (Fig. 2 E). In contrast, Ru4+ (0.62 Å) [27] induces localized lattice distortion owing to its smaller ionic radius, as evidenced in region 2 (Fig. 2 F and G). This distortion generates localized strain fields that modulate the spatial distribution of electrochemically active sites, effectively optimizing their accessibility. Remarkably, Ru atoms (atomic radius: 1.25 Å) are intercalated between adjacent Nb5+/Ta5+ columns, triggering significant lattice expansion. Specifically, the (330) interplanar spacing increases to 0.6 nm—more than twice that of the (770) plane (0.25 nm)—resulting in widened interlayer channels. These expanded pathways enable rapid infiltration of Li2SO4 electrolyte into the lattice interstices, which directly enhances the specific capacitance and high-rate performance of the supercapacitor by improving ion diffusion kinetics and charge storage efficiency.

Fig. 2 H and I depict the atomic-scale strain distributions within the composite. Strain analysis reveals anisotropic lattice deformations, characterized by tensile strain (red-yellow regions) and compressive strain (green–blue regions) [28]. The tensile strain originates from Ru4+ substitution-induced lattice distortion, while the compressive strain arises from steric hindrance between larger Nb5+/Ta5+ ions (ionic radius: 0.64 Å) and smaller Ru4+ ions (0.62 Å). Complementary HAADF-STEM imaging (Fig. 2 J) further demonstrates contrast variations induced by surface modification, where low-Z elements (C, N, B, P) derived from HBP-GQD are visualized as dark regions due to their reduced electron scattering efficiency. Elemental mapping confirms the homogeneous spatial distribution of all constituent elements (Nb, Ta, Ru, O, C, N, B, P), with C/N/B/P signals exclusively localized within the HBP-GQD domains (Fig. 2 L-R). This spatially resolved chemical homogeneity provides direct evidence for the successful integration of HBP-GQD into the composite framework, ensuring synergistic interfacial interactions between the conductive carbon matrix and metal oxide nanosheets.

Table S1 displays the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) results, which validate the stoichiometric composition of the composite. The measured elemental ratio of Nb: Ta: Ru = 0.85: 0.1: 0.15 aligns closely with the nominal formula (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5, with minor deviations attributable to synthesis dynamics. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) under an O2 atmosphere (1000 °C, 5 h) quantifies the carbonaceous content as 68.8 wt%, corresponding to 31.8 wt% (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5. These analytical results collectively validate the precise control over both structural and compositional parameters in the synthesized material, demonstrating a well-balanced integration of the conductive carbon matrix (HBP-GQD) and the metal oxide framework.

Fig. 3
A and B display X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern crystal structure projection along c-axe of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD. Seventeen distinct diffraction peaks are observed at 24.8°, 27.8°, 28.9°, 29.9°, 31.7°, 33.2°, 34.1°, 36.7°, 38°, 39.7°, 41.9°, 48.6°, 54.1°, 57.3°, 59.6°, 64.6°, and 66.8°, which are indexed to the (440), (600), (301), (620), (710), (521), (800), (541), (750), (721), (770), (202), (442), (990), (1330), (930), and (1022) crystallographic planes of Nb2O5 (JCPDS No. 072–1484). The absence of impurity peaks (e.g., Ta2O5 or RuO2 phases) confirms that Ta and Ru atoms are substitutionally incorporated into the Nb2O5 lattice without phase segregation. Crystallographic analysis (Fig. 3 B) reveals a long-range ordered structure composed of interconnected octahedral units forming “head-to-head” tunnel architectures. These aligned tunnels provide low-resistance pathways for electrolyte penetration during charge/discharge cycles, significantly enhancing interfacial charge transfer kinetics and overall electrochemical performance.

Fig. 3 C compares electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD and (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5-HBP-GQD. A significant disparity in EPR signal intensity is observed between the two composites, as shown in Fig. 3 C. The (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD exhibits a weak EPR signal (g-factor≈2.002), indicative of minimal oxygen vacancy concentration. In contrast, the Ru-modified composite displays a pair of intense EPR peaks at the same g-value, unambiguously confirming the presence of abundant oxygen vacancies. This remarkable enhancement in oxygen vacancy density is directly attributed to the incorporation of Ru4+ ions. Specifically, the substitution of Nb5+ with lower-valent Ru4+ creates charge imbalance within the lattice, which is compensated through the formation of oxygen vacancies via the Kröger-Vink defect equilibrium [29]. This self-doping mechanism not only stabilizes the crystal structure but also optimizes electronic conductivity by introducing additional charge carriers, thereby enhancing the material's electrochemical activity.

Fig. 3 D presents Raman spectrum of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD. Two prominent Raman bands are observed at 1330 cm−1 (D-band) and 1552 cm−1 (G-band), corresponding to sp3-hybridized disordered carbon and sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon in HBP-GQD, respectively. A weaker vibrational mode at 650 cm−1 can be assigned to symmetric stretching of metal–oxygen (M−O, M = Nb, Ta, Ru) bonds within oxide framework. The introduction of Ru4+ ions induces a redshift in the M−O Raman peak, attributed to their strong electron-withdrawing effect, which weakens the adjacent Nb-O and Ta-O bonds through localized electron density redistribution. Furthermore, the intensity ratio of D-band to G-band (ID/IG) is calculated as 1.45, reflecting a high density of structural defects in the carbon matrix. Compared to (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5-HBP-GQD reference (Fig. S4), both D- and G-bands in the Ru-modified composite exhibit significant redshifts, suggesting an enhanced electronic coupling between metal oxide nanocrystal and HBP-GQD support [30]. This interfacial interaction likely facilitates charge transfer across heterojunction, contributing to improved electrochemical activity.

Fig. 3 E compares Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD and pristine HBP-GQD. The composite exhibits six characteristic absorption bands at 3413 cm−1 (O–H/N–H/=C–H stretching), 2923 cm−1 (−C–H stretching), 1697 cm−1 (C=O stretching), 1628 cm−1 (aromatic C=C stretching), 1392 cm−1 (C-N/X-O stretching), and 1045 cm−1 (C-O/C-B/P-O in-plane bending). The spectral features of the composite closely align with those of pristine HBP-GQD, confirming the retention of key functional groups (e.g., carbonyl, aromatic, and heteroatom-containing moieties) during thermal annealing. Notably, the absence of new absorption bands and the preservation of peak positions indicate that the HBP-GQD structure remains chemically stable upon integration with the metal oxide framework. This structural integrity ensures effective electron delocalization across the composite, which is critical for maintaining high electrical conductivity and catalytic activity.

XPS characterization revealed electron transfer and chemical state changes in non-metallic elements (Fig. 4
A): (1) The C1s spectrum showed a 0.3 eV positive binding energy shift (284.8 → 285.1 eV) after Ru doping, indicating electron transfer from the HBP-GQD carbon matrix to the metal oxide framework (Fig. 4 B) [31]. (2) Deconvolution of the O1s spectrum resolved lattice oxygen (Olatt, 530.7 eV), oxygen vacancy-adsorbed oxygen (Oads, 531.8 eV), and physisorbed water (Ow, 533.2 eV). The Oads fraction increased from 39.8 % to 57.1 %, confirming Ru doping-induced oxygen vacancy enrichment, consistent with EPR results (Fig. 4 C). (3) The B-O bond (191.4 eV) in the B1s spectrum shifted 0.5 eV to 191.9 eV, accompanied by the emergence of M−B bonds (Nb/Ta/Ru-B, 188.1 eV), demonstrating electron donation from B to metals (Fig. 4 D) [32]. (4) The P2p spectrum exhibited a 0.3 eV shift in the P-O bond (134.1 eV) with concurrent M−P bond formation, reflecting P → M electron transfer (Fig. 4 E). (5) The M−N bond (399.2 eV) in the N 1 s spectrum shifted −0.6 eV, driven by nitrogen’s higher electronegativity (3.04), which facilitated electron back-donation from metals (M) to N, contrasting with the B/P electron transfer trends (Fig. 4 F).

The oxidation states and electronic reconstruction mechanisms of metallic elements were elucidated: (1) In the Nb 3d spectrum, Nb4+ (207.6/210.5 eV) and Nb5+ (208.4/210.8 eV) exhibited a + 0.2 eV binding energy shift compared to the undoped material (Fig. 4 G). (2) Similarly, Ta4+ (26.4/28.4 eV) and Ta5+ (26.8/28.7 eV) in the Ta 4f spectrum shifted + 0.2 eV, indicating reduced electron density at Nb/Ta sites (Fig. 4 H). (3) The Ru 3p spectrum resolved Ru0 (461.8/484 eV), Ru4+ (463.6/486.9 eV), and satellite peaks (470.4/497.4 eV), confirming Ru’s mixed valence states. Due to Ru’s higher electronegativity (2.2 vs. Nb: 1.6, Ta: 1.5), electrons transferred from Nb/Ta to Ru via Ru-O-M (M=Nb/Ta) bridges, depleting electron density at metal sites (Fig. 4 I). This process simultaneously induced C-Nb bond formation (204.5/206.8 eV), strengthening interfacial coupling.

The results of XPS analysis demonstrate that Ru doping drives charge redistribution via electronegativity gradients, achieving triple regulatory effects: (1) Oxygen vacancy enrichment (Oads ratio: 57.1 %) enhances active site density; (2) Optimization of metal d-band electronic structures (Nb/Ta binding energy shifts) reduces activation barriers for oxygen species; (3) Heterointerface electron delocalization (C → M, M → Ru) accelerates charge transfer kinetics. These synergistic effects significantly enhance the electrochemical activity of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD, providing a theoretical foundation for designing high-performance supercapacitor electrode materials.

To evaluate impact of H2 and O2 evolution on charge–discharge processes in an aqueous electrolyte, the electrochemical behavior of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD electrode in 1 M Li2SO4 solution was systematically investigated by chronoamperometry and cyclic voltammetry (CV). Fig. 5
A displays the chronoamperometric curves recorded at varying applied voltages, revealing distinct voltage-dependent trends. At potentials below −0.9 V, the current density remains below 0.1 A g−1, indicating negligible H2 evolution. In contrast, a sharp increase in current density (>0.1 A g−1) is observed when the voltage exceeds −0.9 V, confirming significant interference from H2 evolution. Consequently, a lower voltage limit of −0.9 V was selected to suppress parasitic reactions. Fig. 5 B further illustrates the kinetic characteristics in the high-potential region. Below 0.8 V, the CV profiles exhibit stable closed-loop behavior at the high-potential terminus, suggesting effective suppression of O2 evolution. However, a rapid current surge emerges in the anodic branch at potentials above 0.8 V, indicating dominant O2 evolution. Accordingly, an upper voltage limit of 0.8 V was established. By integrating these findings, a stable electrochemical window of 1.7 V (−0.9 to 0.8 V) was determined for the electrode. This optimized voltage range minimizes gas evolution interference, providing a reliable operational window for subsequent quasi-equilibrium studies.

Fig. 5 C and D present the galvanostatic charge–discharge profiles and specific capacitance values (measured at 1 A g−1) for five electrodes: (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD, (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-H-GQD, (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HB-GQD, (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-GQD, and Ru-free (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5-HBP-GQD. All discharge curves exhibit a two-stage voltage decay: a gradual decline associated with electric double-layer capacitance (EDLC) and a rapid drop linked to pseudo-capacitance. The specific capacitances follow a clear hierarchy: (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD (1157.5F g−1) > HB-GQD (837.4F g−1) > H-GQD (755.3F g−1) > GQD (542.9F g−1) > Ru-free variant (335.7F g−1), revealing three critical insights. First, the incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) significantly enhances capacitance compared to the pristine (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5 electrode (218.5F g−1) (Fig. 5 D), attributed to reduced particle size (shortening electron/ion transport pathways) and improved electrical conductivity (accelerating reaction kinetics). Second, GQD structural design critically dictates performance; HBP-functionalized GQDs yield a capacitance over twofold higher than non-functionalized GQDs, highlighting the importance of tailored interfacial engineering for optimizing metal oxide electrodes. Third, Ru incorporation drives a threefold capacitance increase via dual mechanisms: (1) Ru4+ ions embedded in the Nb2O5 lattice enhance intrinsic conductivity, and (2) atomically dispersed Ru⁰ within interlayer tunnels creates interconnected conductive pathways and leverages Ru’s high redox capacity. This synergistic integration of GQD architecture and Ru doping establishes a robust strategy for maximizing charge storage kinetics and capacitance in advanced electrode materials.

Fig. 5 E-F and Fig. S5 depict the galvanostatic charge–discharge profiles and specific capacitance versus current density plots (1–50 A g–1) for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-based electrodes with varying GQD architectures and Ru content in 1 M Li2SO4 electrolyte. universal trend is observed: specific capacitance decreases with increasing current density due to insufficient time for complete electrochemical reactions during rapid discharge. The Ru-free (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5-HBP-GQD electrode exhibits the poorest rate capability, with capacitance plummeting from 335.6F g−1 at 1 A g−1 to 132.3F g−1 at 50 A g−1. In contrast, Ru incorporation markedly enhances rate performance. For instance, (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD electrode delivers 553.8F g−1 at 10 A g−1, significantly outperforming its Ru-free counterpart. Notably, GQD structural design critically governs high-rate behavior. (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD variant demonstrates exceptional performance, achieving 1157.5F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and retaining 553.8F g−1 (10 A g−1) and 403.5F g−1 (50 A g−1). This hierarchy—HBP-GQD > HB-GQD > H-GQD > GQD—underscores the pivotal role of HBP-GQD in constructing robust conductive networks and optimizing charge transfer kinetics. These results conclusively establish HBP-GQD as the optimal modifier for engineering high-performance (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5 nanocrystals, enabling superior energy storage under high-power conditions.

To evaluate the electrochemical performance of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD, a symmetrical supercapacitor was assembled using identical electrodes and a PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte. Fig. 6
A displays cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves measured across varying voltage ranges. At potentials below 1.7 V, the CV profiles exhibit quasi-rectangular shapes with minimal distortion, suggesting negligible oxygen evolution. In contrast, a pronounced anodic surge emerges above 1.7 V, indicative of accelerated O2 evolution that compromises electrochemical stability. Consequently, a safe operational window of 1.7 V was selected to mitigate parasitic reactions and ensure reliable measurements. Further, Fig. 6 B indicates the CV current rapidly increases with the increase of scan rate, revealing an excellent conductivity.

Fig. 6 C and D present galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) profiles at incremental current densities and the corresponding specific capacitance trends. The specific capacitance decreases from 341.4F g−1 at 1 A g−1 to 155.5F g−1 (10 A g−1) and 98.8F g−1 (50 A g−1), reflecting kinetic limitations at elevated rates. This attenuation arises from insufficient time for complete redox reactions of the multimetallic species (Nb, Ta, Ru) during rapid discharge, as ion diffusion and charge-transfer kinetics lag behind the applied current. Despite this, the composite retains 29 % of its initial capacitance at 50 A g−1, underscoring robust rate capability attributable to its hierarchical pore structure and conductive GQD network.

The Ragone plot in Fig. 6 E highlights the device’s energy-power balance. A maximum energy density of 137.0 Wh kg−1 is achieved at 850 W kg−1, rivaling commercial lithium-ion batteries. Remarkably, 62.4 Wh kg−1 and 39.7 Wh kg−1 are retained at 8.5 kW kg−1 and 42.5 kW kg−1, respectively, demonstrating exceptional performance in high-power scenarios such as rapid energy delivery systems.

Long-term stability was assessed via 10,000 GCD cycles at 5 A g−1 (Fig. 6 F). The specific capacitance declines minimally from 208.2F g−1 to 205.5F g−1 (98.7 % retention), with no structural degradation observed via post-cycling characterization. This stability stems from the synergistic effects of the robust (Nb, Ta, Ru)-oxide framework, which resists phase separation, and the HBP-GQD matrix that buffers volume changes during cycling.

To validate the applicability of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD composite in flexible electronics, systematic mechanical deformation tests were conducted (Fig. 7 ). The symmetrical supercapacitor retained 98.9 % and 95.6 % of its initial specific capacitance (208.2F g−1) after 50 cycles (5 A g−1) under extreme 180° folding and 360° twisting deformations, respectively, demonstrating exceptional electrochemical stability. This performance originates from the hierarchical structural design: the ductile HBP-GQD matrix buffers mechanical stress through its flexible network, while the interconnected (Nb, Ta, Ru)-oxide framework preserves the integrity of ionic/electronic transport pathways. Cross-sectional SEM analysis confirmed the absence of microcracks or interfacial delamination post-deformation (inset, Fig. 7). Compared to carbon nanotube-based flexible devices, the composite exhibits a 35 % improvement in capacitance retention, highlighting the synergistic enhancement of interfacial adhesion and phase-separation resistance by the multimetallic oxide-GQD hybrid. This material shows promising potential for wearable applications such as foldable displays and epidermal sensors. Future studies will focus on long-term mechanical-electrochemical coupled testing (>10,000 cycles) and performance evaluation under complex environmental conditions (e.g., temperature/humidity variations) to advance its practical deployment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of HBP-GQD and Ru in improving the supercapacitor performance
ce:section.ce:para:
To systematically unravel the roles of graphene quantum dot (GQD) derivatives and Ru doping in tailoring morphology and crystallinity of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5, four functionalized GQD variants—pristine GQD, histidine-functionalized GQD (H-GQD), boron-doped GQD (HB-GQD), and boron-phosphorus co-doped GQD (HBP-GQD)—were integrated into the oxide matrix. In the absence of HBP-GQD, our investigation shows that Ru cannot be doped into Nb2O5 nanocrystals. the resulting product is composed of single Ru nanoparticles and Ta-doped Nb2O5 nanoparticles. This is due to the existence of a significant difference in ionic radius and diffusion rate of Nb5+ and Ru3+. In addition, Ru3+ is easy to be reduced to Ru0 during the thermal annealing due to its lower reducing potential. These factors lead to severe segregation, ultimately resulting in formation of single Ru nanoparticles and single Ta-doped Nb2O5 nanoparticles. However, in the presence of GQD Nb5+ and Ru3+ are combined with GQD to form water-soluble metal complexes. The strong coordination between Ru3+ and Nb5+ and GQD ensure the uniform dispersion of Nb5+ and Ru3+ at atom level in a solution state and a solid state. This leads to the formation of Ta/Ru-doped Nb2O5 nanocrystals without Ru nanoparticles. Interestingly, comprehensive SEM and XRD analyses (Fig. 8
A-F) reveal a striking correlation between GQD functionality, Ru incorporation, and the resultant nanostructure evolution. As depicted in Fig. 8 A-D, the structural hierarchy of GQDs governs the morphological transitions of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5. The composite with pristine GQD (Fig. 8 A) exhibits irregular spherical nanoparticles (50–100 nm) with severe aggregation, attributed to weak interfacial interactions. Introducing H-GQD (Fig. 8 B) induces a hybrid architecture of nanoparticles and emerging nanoflakes, signaling a kinetic shift from isotropic to anisotropic growth. HB-GQD (Fig. 8 C) further amplifies this trend, yielding high-aspect-ratio nanoflakes (length/thickness > 10:1) through enhanced facet-specific adsorption. Remarkably, HBP-GQD (Fig. 8 D) stabilizes quadrilateral nanosheets with near-unity aspect ratios (∼1:1), underscoring the critical role of boron-phosphorus co-doping in templating planar growth. Further, XRD patterns (Fig. 8 F) provide crystallographic evidence for structural differentiation. The HBP-GQD composite displays sharp, intense peaks (e.g., (440) at 2θ = 24.8° and (770) at 2θ = 41.9°), indicative of high crystallinity and preferential orientation along specific facets. In stark contrast, the Ru-free analogue (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD (Fig. 8 E) exhibits broadened, low-intensity peaks and disordered spherical aggregates (∼200 nm), confirming Ru’s dual role in enhancing crystallinity and suppressing particle coalescence. The absence of Ru disrupts metal-GQD coordination, leading to uncontrolled nucleation and amorphous phase formation, as evidenced by the weakened (001) peak intensity.

To probe the metal-GQD interaction dynamics, the electrical conductivity measurements of precursor solutions were conducted. The pristine system (no GQD) exhibits high conductivity (6.8 μS cm−1), reflecting abundant free Nb5+/Ta5+/Ru3+ ions. Progressive conductivity reduction (4.6 → 3.2 μS cm−1) across GQD, H-GQD, HB-GQD, and HBP-GQD systems highlights escalating metal-ion chelation efficiency. The ultra-low conductivity in the HBP-GQD system (3.2 μS cm−1) underscores its superior coordination capacity, driven by boron/phosphorus-induced electron deficiency, which facilitates near-complete conversion of metal ions into neutral Me-GQD complexes. This minimizes Ostwald ripening and enables precise nanostructure control [33].

The observed structural modulation arises from two intertwined mechanisms: (1) facet-selective coordination stabilization: Strong Me–O/N bonds anchor GQDs to high-energy (101) facets of Nb2O5, selectively inhibiting vertical growth and promoting lateral expansion. (2) adsorption-driven anisotropic growth: Differential GQD adsorption affinities across crystallographic planes (e.g., HBP-GQD’s preferential binding to (770) facets) direct anisotropic growth. For instance, HBP-GQD’s electron-deficient surface enhances interactions with high-energy facets, fostering interconnected tunnel structures critical for ion diffusion. This work establishes HBP-GQD and Ru doping as pivotal tools for engineering high-surface-area, crystallographically aligned nanostructures. The synergistic interplay between GQD functionalization and Ru doping not only refines morphology but also optimizes charge transfer pathways, addressing key limitations in supercapacitor electrode materials. The proposed methodology offers a universal paradigm for designing transition-metal oxide composites with programmable architectures, paving the way for next-generation energy storage devices.

To investigate the synergistic effects of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and Ru doping on conductivity, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and lithium-ion diffusion kinetics were systematically analyzed in 1 M Li2SO4 electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 9
A and Table S2, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5 decreased dramatically from 418.8 Ω to 2.1 Ω upon HBP-GQD modification, corresponding to a 199.4-fold enhancement in Rct value. Intermediate composites—(Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-GQD (248.2 Ω), (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-H-GQD (34.87 Ω), and (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HB-GQD (10 Ω)—demonstrate that hierarchical structural modifications, including histidine functionalization, boron-phosphorus co-doping, and biochar integration, critically optimize interfacial charge transfer kinetics. Notably, Ru doping alone reduced Rct by over 118.2-fold, attributed to the formation of interconnected tunnel structures embedded with Ru atoms, which facilitate rapid electron transport through their intrinsic high conductivity. However, the Rct value does not accurately reflect the electrical conductivity of the materials because the semicircle of Nyquist plots in the high-frequency region is related to both electron transfer and ion diffusion. To resolve the problem, one classical four-probe method was employed for measuring electrical conductivities of (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5, (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5-HBO-GQD and (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD. The results show that the electrical conductivities are 8.7 × 10-6 s cm−1 for (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5, 9.1 × 10-2 s cm−1 for (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5-HBP-GQD, and 5.3 × 10-1 s cm−1 for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD. These data demonstrate that the introduction of Ru and HBP-GQD achieves to 60919.54 times enhancement in electrical conductivity.

To further elucidate the impact of HBP-GQD and Ru doping on the ion diffusion, the linear correlation between the imaginary impedance component (Z″) and the square root of frequency (f1/2) was analyzed (Fig. 9 B) [34]. Slope-derived lithium-ion diffusion coefficients (DLi +) exhibited a progressive enhancement across the series: 3.4 × 10−12 cm2 s−1 for pristine(Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5, 2.77 × 10−11 cm2 s−1 for (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5-HBP-GQD, 4.0 × 10−10 cm2 s−1 for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-GQD, 6.5 × 10−11 cm2 s−1 for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-H-GQD, 2.5 × 10−10 cm2 s−1 for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HB-GQD, and 1.1 × 10−9 cm2 s−1 for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD (Fig. 9 C). This 309.5-fold improvement in DLi + underscores the dual role of Ru doping and HBP-GQD integration: Ru optimizes bulk conductivity through tailored atomic architectures, while HBP-GQD’s multi-functional surface engineering (e.g., heteroatom doping and biochar synergy) significantly lowers interfacial resistance and accelerates ion diffusion. These findings establish a paradigm for designing high-performance electrodes through synergistic electronic and ionic pathway engineering.

To evaluate the exposure of electrochemically active sites, the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of the electrodes was systematically investigated via CV in 0.1 M KNO3 electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 10
A, the (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5 and Ru-free (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5-HBP-GQD electrode exhibited the lowest current density, indicative of limited electrochemically active sites. In contrast, Ru doping induced a remarkable enhancement in ECSA. Similarly, electrodes modified with histidine-functionalized and boron and phosphorus-doped GQD derivatives—(Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-GQD, (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-H-GQD, (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HB-GQD, and (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD—demonstrated progressively elevated current densities (Fig. 10 B–E), confirming that the integration of tailored GQDs significantly enhances the accessibility of active sites. Notably, the (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD electrode achieved the highest CV current density, reflecting an optimized ECSA.

Quantitative analysis of ECSA was conducted by correlating the current density variation (ΔJ) with scan rates (Fig. 10 F). Linear regression yielded slope values of 0.00016 for (Nb0.7Ta0.3)2O5-HBP-GQD, 0.0003 for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-GQD, 0.0055 for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-H-GQD, 0.0083 for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HB-GQD, and 0.0135 for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD. Given the direct proportionality between the slope and ECSA for analogous nanomaterials [35], these data reveal a striking 84.4-fold improvement in ECSA for (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD compared to Ru-free counterpart. Collectively, these findings underscore the synergistic interplay between HBP-GQD functionalization and Ru doping in expanding electrochemically active interfaces, offering a novel strategy for designing high-performance electrodes.

To elucidate charge storage mechanism of the (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD, cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis was systematically performed at scan rates ranging from 5 to 40 mV s–1 in a 1.0 M Li2SO4 electrolyte (Fig. 11
A). The CV profiles reveal well-defined redox peaks within a stable potential window of 1.7 V (−0.9 to 0.8 V), indicative of sequential oxidation/reduction reactions involving Nb, Ta, and Ru species within the composite matrix (Fig. 11 A and Fig. S6). The progressive enhancement of current densities at elevated scan rates demonstrates superior electrode kinetics, ascribed to the material's high electrical conductivity and optimized ion transport pathways.
The charge storage dynamics were further quantified using the power-law relationship [36]:
i = avb (1).

where the derived b-values of 0.66 (cathodic) and 0.67 (anodic) from log(i) versus log(v) plots (Fig. 10B) signify a hybrid charge storage mechanism. These intermediate b-values (0.5 < b < 1.0) confirm the coexistence of diffusion-controlled Faradaic processes and surface-mediated capacitive contributions. To deconvolute these mechanisms, the total current response was partitioned using:

where k1, k2 and k3 represent contributions from electric double-layer capacitance (EDLC), surface redox pseudo-capacitance, and diffusion-controlled bulk redox capacitance, respectively. As shown in Fig. 11 C, diffusion-controlled processes dominate at low scan rates (67.1 % at 5 mV s–1), emphasizing the critical role of Li+ intercalation into the (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD lattice. This contribution diminishes at higher scan rates due to kinetic limitations in Li+ diffusion through the composite's interconnected tunnels. Conversely, EDLC and surface redox contributions increase significantly with scan rate, leveraging the three-dimensional carbon framework for rapid interfacial charge transfer. Notably, the diffusion-controlled capacitance (211.16F g−1 at 5 mV s−1) consistently exceeds surface contributions by factors of 23.3 (surface redox) and 2.2 (EDLC) across all tested rates (Fig. 11 D), underscoring the material's structural superiority in balancing bulk and interfacial charge storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with Other Nb2O5-Based electrode materials
ce:section.ce:para: For a systematic evaluation, the supercapacitor performance metrics of Nb2O5-based electrode materials are summarized in Table 1 . The (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD composite exhibits superior performance compared to all listed counterparts, underscoring its potential for advanced energy storage applications. This exceptional performance originates from the following synergistic mechanisms:

Firstly, coordination-driven synthesis and structural optimization: The synthesis of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD involves a coordination-based strategy, where HBP-GQD acts as multifunctional ligands. The strong chelation between HBP-GQDs and metal ions (Nb5+, Ta5+, Ru3+) ensures atomic-level homogeneity in the precursor, enabling uniform distribution of Nb, Ta, and Ru during crystallization. Furthermore, HBP-GQDs selectively adsorb onto specific crystallographic planes of Nb2O5, directing anisotropic growth to form a well-defined sheet-like nanostructure. This dual role of HBP-GQD as coordination templates and growth modifiers—yields a hierarchically ordered architecture with high crystallinity, interconnected ion diffusion channels, and abundant electrochemically active sites. The shortened electron/ion transport pathways and enhanced structural stability collectively contribute to improved specific capacitance and rate capability.

Secondly, defect engineering via oxygen vacancies: The self-doping of low-valent metal ions (Nb4+/3+, Ta4+/3+, Ru3+) during synthesis introduces a high density of oxygen vacancies. These vacancies generate defect energy levels within the bandgap of Nb2O5, effectively narrowing its electronic bandgap and elevating bulk conductivity. The increased carrier density facilitates rapid charge transfer kinetics, significantly enhancing high-rate performance.

Thirdly, surface modification and interface engineering: The integration of HBP-GQDs on the composite surface establishes a semiconductor heterojunction at the (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD interface. This heterojunction reduces the interfacial charge transfer resistance by aligning energy bands, thereby accelerating electron transport. Additionally, the hydrophilic functional groups (–COOH, –OH) on HBP-GQD improve electrolyte wettability, ensuring stable ion adsorption/desorption at the electrode–electrolyte interface during prolonged cycling. The synergy between enhanced electronic conductivity and optimized interfacial dynamics further bolsters rate capability.

Fourthly, atomic-level design with Ru incorporation: The strategic embedding of Ru atoms within the Nb2O5 lattice introduces two critical advantages. First, Ru4+ exhibits a high theoretical pseudocapacitance due to its multiple redox states and near-100 % atomic utilization efficiency. Second, Ru atoms create interconnected conductive pathways within the oxide matrix, which lowers charge transfer impedance and promotes rapid Faradaic reactions. These effects synergistically elevate both specific capacitance and rate performance.

Fifthly, (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD composite achieves unprecedented supercapacitor performance through a multi-scale design strategy, integrating coordination chemistry, defect engineering, surface modification, and atomic-level structural tuning. This work provides a paradigm for developing advanced oxide-based electrodes via rational compositional and architectural control.

An advanced (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5-HBP-GQD electrode material has been successfully constructed by introducing Ta, Ru and HBP-GQD. The study reveals the nanostructure and electrochemical property of (Nb0.7Ta0.15Ru0.15)2O5 depends on the structure of graphene quantum dot and Ru doping. The strong coordination between HBP-GQD and metal ions firmly suppress the structural homogeneity in the precursors and results in the formation of long range ordered Nb2O5 nanocrystals with interconnected tunnel reactors embedded with Ru atoms. The introduction of HBP-GQD and Ru achieves to significant increase in ion diffusion efficiency, electrical conductivity, electrochemically active area, and structural stability. The interconnected tunnel reactors embedded with Ru atoms generates a high capacitance due to high capacity and atomic utilization and improves the electrical conductivity. Unique structure brings an outstanding supercapacitor performance, including high specific capacitance, high-rate capacity, cycling stability. This work provides a paradigm for designing high-performance metal oxide nanomaterials via quantum dot-mediated crystallization and multi-element synergy, with broad implications for energy storage, catalysis, and biosensing.

Li Xiaoshan: Methodology, Investigation. Li Ruiyi: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Yang Yongqiang: Validation, Investigation. Li Zaijun: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
